terms.definition: A feature or behaviour that helps a living thing survive in its environment.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Cacti have adaptations like thick stems to store water in the desert.
terms.common_errors: Thinking adaptation is a conscious choice

terms.definition: The continents of North America and South America considered together.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Americas stretch from the Arctic in the north to the tip of South America.

terms.definition: Relating to the region around the South Pole, characterised by extreme cold and ice.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Antarctic climate zone is the coldest on Earth.
terms.common_errors: Confusing Antarctic with Arctic

terms.definition: A large naturally occurring community of plants and animals adapted to a particular climate, such as desert or rainforest.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The tropical rainforest biome receives high rainfall and has great biodiversity.
terms.common_errors: Confusing biome with ecosystem; biomes are larger

terms.definition: A typical or distinguishing quality or feature of a place or environment.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: One characteristic of a desert is very low rainfall.

terms.definition: A region of the world with a particular pattern of weather conditions, determined by latitude and other factors.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The UK is in the temperate climate zone, with mild weather.

terms.definition: The process by which water vapour in the air cools and turns back into liquid water droplets, forming clouds.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin condensare, to make thick
terms.example_usage: Condensation is the stage of the water cycle where clouds form.
terms.common_errors: Confusing condensation with evaporation

terms.definition: A line on a map joining points of equal height above sea level, showing the shape and steepness of the land.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Closely spaced contour lines on a map indicate steep terrain.

terms.definition: A large urban area formed when several towns or cities have grown together.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Greater Manchester is a conurbation made up of several towns and cities.

terms.definition: A pair of numbers used to specify the exact position of a point on a map using a grid reference system.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin coordinare, to arrange together
terms.example_usage: The coordinates 52N, 1W tell us the exact location on the map.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the verb to coordinate

terms.definition: Relating to the beliefs, customs, arts, and way of life of a particular group of people.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Cultural differences between countries include language, food, and traditions.

terms.definition: A unit of measurement for angles, latitude, and longitude, shown by the symbol.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The equator is at zero degrees latitude.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with temperature degrees or academic degrees

terms.definition: The laying down of material such as sand, silt, or pebbles that has been carried by water, wind, or ice.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin deponere, to put down
terms.example_usage: Deposition at the mouth of a river can form a delta.
terms.common_errors: Confusing deposition with erosion

terms.definition: A dry area of land that receives very little rainfall, often with extreme temperatures.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Sahara is the worlds largest hot desert.
terms.common_errors: Thinking all deserts are hot; some are cold

terms.definition: The way in which something is spread out or arranged across an area.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The distribution of rainfall across the UK is uneven, with more in the west.

terms.definition: The area of land where all the water drains into one particular river system.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The drainage basin of the River Thames covers a large area of southern England.

terms.definition: The first part of a grid reference, reading left to right along the bottom of a map.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Always read the easting before the northing when giving a grid reference.
terms.common_errors: Getting easting and northing the wrong way round

terms.definition: Work and industry that produces goods or services, contributing to the wealth of a region or country.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Farming, manufacturing, and tourism are all types of economic activity.

terms.definition: A community of living things and their physical environment, interacting as a system.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: A pond is a small ecosystem where plants, insects, and fish all depend on each other.
terms.common_errors: Confusing ecosystem with habitat

terms.definition: The height of a point above sea level, often shown by contour lines on a map.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The elevation of the mountain peak is 1,345 metres above sea level.

terms.definition: The power used to run machines, heat buildings, and provide light, derived from various sources.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Wind turbines convert wind energy into electricity.

terms.definition: The wearing away and removal of rock, soil, or land surface by water, wind, ice, or gravity.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin erodere, to eat away
terms.example_usage: Erosion by the sea has created the cliffs along the coast.
terms.common_errors: Confusing erosion with weathering, which breaks rock without moving it

terms.definition: Relating to the continent of Europe, its countries, or its peoples.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: France and Germany are European countries close to the United Kingdom.

terms.definition: The process by which liquid water is heated and turns into water vapour, rising into the atmosphere.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin evaporare, to turn to vapour
terms.example_usage: Evaporation from the sea is the first stage of the water cycle.
terms.common_errors: Confusing evaporation with condensation

terms.definition: Goods or services sold and sent to another country.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin exportare, to carry out
terms.example_usage: Oil is a major export of countries in the Middle East.
terms.common_errors: Confusing export (sending out) with import (bringing in)

terms.definition: A flat area of land on either side of a river that is prone to flooding when the river overflows.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The flood plain is very fertile because of the silt deposited by floods.

terms.definition: A grid reference using four numbers to locate a square on a map.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The four-figure grid reference 2347 tells us which square the church is in.

terms.definition: A set of numbers used to identify a precise location on an Ordnance Survey or similar map.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We used a six-figure grid reference to find the exact location of the bridge.
terms.common_errors: Reading eastings and northings in the wrong order

terms.definition: A very small settlement, smaller than a village, typically without a church or shops.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Old French hamelet, small village
terms.example_usage: A hamlet is the smallest type of settlement and may have only a few houses.

terms.definition: One half of the Earth, divided either into Northern/Southern (by the equator) or Eastern/Western (by the Prime Meridian).
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The United Kingdom is in the Northern Hemisphere.

terms.definition: The study of how people interact with their environment, including settlement, industry, and population.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Human geography looks at where people live and how they use the land.
terms.common_errors: Confusing human geography with physical geography

terms.definition: Goods or services bought from another country and brought in.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin importare, to bring in
terms.example_usage: The UK imports bananas because they cannot grow in its climate.
terms.common_errors: Confusing import with export

terms.definition: The process by which rainwater soaks into the soil and underlying rock.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin in + filtrare, to strain through
terms.example_usage: After rain, infiltration moves water from the surface down into the ground.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the security meaning of infiltration

terms.definition: Imaginary vertical lines on a map or globe measuring distance east or west of the Prime Meridian.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Lines of longitude run from the North Pole to the South Pole.
terms.common_errors: Confusing longitude (vertical) with latitude (horizontal)

terms.definition: A winding curve or bend in a river, typically found in the middle and lower course.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From the River Meander in Turkey, famous for its winding path
terms.example_usage: The river has formed large meanders as it flows across the flat flood plain.

terms.definition: A line of longitude; the Prime Meridian at Greenwich is the reference point at 0 degrees.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Prime Meridian passes through Greenwich in London.

terms.definition: A naturally occurring substance found in rocks and soil, often extracted for human use.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Iron ore is a mineral that has been mined for thousands of years.

terms.definition: The point where a river flows into the sea, a lake, or another river.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The mouth of the River Thames is where it flows into the North Sea.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the body part

terms.definition: The system of numbered grid lines covering Ordnance Survey maps of the United Kingdom.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The National Grid allows us to give accurate grid references for any location in the UK.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the electricity National Grid

terms.definition: A material found in nature that is useful to people, such as water, wood, minerals, or soil.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Water is a natural resource that is essential for farming and drinking.

terms.definition: The second part of a grid reference, reading upwards from the bottom of a map.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: After finding the easting, read the northing to complete the grid reference.

terms.definition: The national mapping agency of Great Britain, producing detailed topographic maps.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Ordnance Survey maps show contour lines, footpaths, and buildings in great detail.

terms.definition: A line of latitude running east-west around the Earth, parallel to the equator.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Tropic of Cancer is a parallel at about 23.5 degrees north.

terms.definition: The study of natural features and processes on the Earths surface, including landforms, weather, and rivers.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Physical geography covers topics like rivers, mountains, and climate.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with human geography

terms.definition: Relating to the areas around the North or South Pole, characterised by extreme cold.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Polar regions have long winters with little or no sunlight.

terms.definition: Water that falls from the atmosphere to the Earths surface as rain, snow, sleet, or hail.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin praecipitare, to fall headlong
terms.example_usage: Precipitation is highest in the mountainous areas of western Britain.
terms.common_errors: Thinking precipitation only means rain

terms.definition: Economic activities that extract raw materials from the Earth, such as farming, fishing, and mining.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Farming is part of the primary sector because it produces food from the land.

terms.definition: The line of zero degrees longitude, passing through Greenwich in London, dividing the Earth into Eastern and Western Hemispheres.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Prime Meridian at Greenwich is the starting point for measuring longitude.

terms.definition: A dense forest found in tropical areas with high rainfall and warm temperatures year-round.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Amazon rainforest is the largest tropical rainforest in the world.
terms.common_errors: Thinking all rainforests are tropical; temperate rainforests also exist

terms.definition: A large area of land that shares common features, such as climate, landscape, or culture.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Lake District is a region known for its mountains and lakes.

terms.definition: The shape and height of the land surface, including hills, valleys, and plains.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The relief of an area is shown by contour lines on a map.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the everyday meaning of relief (feeling better)

terms.definition: Water that flows over the land surface into streams and rivers rather than soaking into the ground.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Heavy rainfall on hard ground leads to increased run-off and can cause flooding.

terms.definition: A tropical grassland with scattered trees, found between tropical rainforest and desert biomes.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Spanish sabana, from Taino zabana
terms.example_usage: The African savannah is home to animals like lions, elephants, and zebras.
terms.common_errors: Spelling it savanna (American) vs savannah (British)

terms.definition: Economic activities that manufacture or process raw materials into finished products.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: A car factory is part of the secondary sector because it turns raw materials into a product.

terms.definition: A grid reference using six numbers to locate a precise point on a map.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: A six-figure grid reference like 234478 pinpoints an exact location within a grid square.

terms.definition: The place where a river begins, usually in high ground such as a spring or bog.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The source of the River Thames is in the Cotswold Hills.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the history meaning of source (evidence)

terms.definition: A point on a map with a number showing its exact height above sea level.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The spot height on the map showed the hilltop was 324 metres above sea level.

terms.definition: Relating to a climate zone with mild temperatures, moderate rainfall, and four distinct seasons.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The United Kingdom has a temperate climate with warm summers and cool winters.

terms.definition: Economic activities that provide services rather than producing goods, such as shops, schools, and hospitals.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin tertiarius, of the third
terms.example_usage: Teaching and nursing are part of the tertiary sector.

terms.definition: A region of the Earth that observes the same standard time, based on longitude.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The UK and France are in different time zones, so their clocks show different times.

terms.definition: Relating to the detailed mapping of the physical features of an area, including contours and landmarks.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: A topographic map shows the height and shape of the land using contour lines.

terms.definition: The buying and selling of goods and services between people, regions, or countries.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: International trade means countries can buy goods they cannot produce themselves.

terms.definition: The movement of eroded material by water, wind, ice, or gravity from one place to another.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Rivers transport sediment from the mountains to the sea.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the general meaning of transport (vehicles)

terms.definition: A smaller river or stream that flows into a larger river.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The River Kennet is a tributary of the River Thames.

terms.definition: Either of the two lines of latitude at about 23.5 degrees north (Cancer) and south (Capricorn) of the equator.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Greek tropikos, of a turn
terms.example_usage: The Tropic of Cancer marks the northern boundary of the tropical zone.

terms.definition: Relating to the warm, wet region between the Tropic of Cancer and the Tropic of Capricorn.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Tropical climates are hot and humid with heavy rainfall.

terms.definition: A cold, treeless biome found in polar regions with frozen ground and low-growing vegetation.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Russian tundra, from Sami tundar, treeless plain
terms.example_usage: The tundra biome is too cold for trees to grow.

terms.definition: The process by which an increasing proportion of a population moves to live in towns and cities.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin urbanus, of the city
terms.example_usage: Urbanisation accelerated during the Industrial Revolution as people moved to factory towns.

terms.definition: A zone of plant life that corresponds to a particular climate zone, running roughly parallel to the equator.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Vegetation belts range from tropical rainforest near the equator to tundra near the poles.

terms.definition: The continuous movement of water between the Earths surface and atmosphere through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The water cycle explains how rain falls, flows into rivers, and eventually evaporates back into the atmosphere.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the water cycle has a start and end; it is continuous
